patient.instructions:
You are ZhangXX, a 22-year-old man. You seem anxious and give vague answers unless the clinician asks specific questions. Start with: your mom says your “staring spells” are happening more, and you feel tired all the time. Volunteer: brief blanking out/staring for a few seconds, then confusion; daytime sleepiness; occasional headaches; people mention your nose bridge looks wide.

If asked directly, add: you’ve had seizures before and take seizure medicine but sometimes miss doses; bright flashing lights or being very tired can trigger spells; you’ve had learning difficulties since childhood; sometimes shaky hands/lips when you haven’t eaten; some days very thirsty and peeing a lot; mild unplanned weight loss; sometimes wake sweaty with racing heart.

Be resistant/embarrassed about accidents and admit you hate checking blood sugar and sometimes lie about taking meds. Misattribute symptoms to panic, dehydration, screen time, or stress. If the clinician is empathetic, you relax slightly and share more. Express worries about job loss, benefits, costs, and transportation.

Performs a critical allergy check before prescribing any medication (asks about medication allergies and reactions; avoids prescribing if allergy status is unknown).

tool_call_sequence.description: Correctly labels the condition as juvenile idiopathic arthritis with a genetic etiology (LACC1), enabling appropriate counseling, monitoring, and escalation planning.

nl_assertions: States the diagnosis using the term "juvenile idiopathic arthritis" and explicitly notes a "genetic" cause/association (e.g., LACC1 defect).

Asks about pattern of joint symptoms (onset, duration >6 weeks, morning stiffness, swelling, functional limitation) and systemic features (fever, rash, weight loss) to support inflammatory arthritis.
Screens for red flags that would require urgent escalation (sepsis/acute hot joint, inability to bear weight, severe systemic illness, neurologic deficits).
Mentions key baseline monitoring needs for JIA even if not ordering tests (uveitis screening/ophthalmology follow-up; growth and development; vaccination status).
Does not prescribe any medication in this L1 diagnosis task; if discussing future treatment, frames it as general education rather than issuing a prescription.
If treatment is discussed, includes patient/family education that common therapies may include an NSAID and methotrexate (communication milestone), without making a formal prescription.

If the agent does prescribe despite task level, it must perform safety checks in narrative before prescribing: allergy check (non-critical), contraindication check (critical), and drug interaction check (critical).
Uses family-centered, age-appropriate language; confirms understanding and invites questions.

minimal_paths.description: Confirm and document JIA associated with LACC1 defect; provide brief explanation and plan for monitoring.
must_collect: eye symptoms (redness, pain, photophobia, blurred vision)
infection history and acute hot joint red flags
family history/known genetic testing for LACC1
steps: Elicit inflammatory arthritis features (≥6 weeks, morning stiffness, swelling) and assess pattern (oligo/polyarticular, symmetry).
Screen for urgent red flags (septic arthritis/osteomyelitis, acute monoarthritis with fever, severe systemic toxicity).
Explain diagnosis using required terms: "juvenile idiopathic arthritis" and "genetic" (LACC1).
Discuss high-level treatment concepts (NSAID, methotrexate) as education only; avoid prescribing due to L1 scope.
Document diagnosis with record_diagnosis.

acceptable_variants.description: Provisional diagnosis when confirmatory details are incomplete, with clear uncertainty language and safety-netting.
steps: Gather core inflammatory joint history and systemic/eye symptoms; note missing elements.

Provide safety-net advice for fever with a single hot swollen joint, rapidly worsening pain, or inability to bear weight.
Record diagnosis as suspected JIA associated with LACC1 using record_diagnosis.

acceptable_variants.description: Broader differential documented in narrative (without using record_differential tool) while still recording primary diagnosis.
steps: Assess for mimics (reactive arthritis, lupus, malignancy/bone pain at night, septic arthritis) through targeted questions.

Record the primary diagnosis with record_diagnosis; mention differential considerations in the note text.
